title: EdgeRelations

tex_statement.html:
Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG.

  1. If $v_1 \sus v_2 \in G$ then we call $v_1$ and $v_2$ adjacent in $G$.
  2. Edges of the form $v_1 \hut v_2$ or $v_1 \huh v_2$ are called into $v_1$.
    Edges of the form $v_1 \tuh v_2$ or $v_1 \huh v_2$ are called into $v_2$.
  3. Edges of the form $v_1 \tuh v_2$ or $v_2 \hut v_1$ are called out of $v_1$.

lean.title: EdgeRelations -- adjacency
lean.comments:
--
-- `Adjacent G v‚āĀ v‚āā` means there is *some* edge of any kind between
-- `v‚āĀ` and `v‚āā` in the CDMG `G`. This is the LN's "$v_1$ and $v_2$
-- are adjacent in $G$" phrasing -- a prose-level name for the `sus`
-- relation of def_3_2.
/-
Verbatim from `lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex` (def 3.3,
item 1):

  If $v_1 \sus v_2 \in G$ then we call $v_1$ and $v_2$ \emph{adjacent
  in $G$}.
-/
--
-- ## Design choice
--
-- * **`def`, not `abbrev` or `notation`.** A `def` gives us a proper
--   identifier we can mention by name in claim statements (claim 3.1
--   reads "no two nodes in $J$ are adjacent") and that appears in
--   goals and error messages. `abbrev` would dissolve into `sus`
--   immediately, losing the readable name. `notation` would
--   introduce a new arrow token, which we don't need -- def_3_2
--   already provides `v‚āĀ ‚Üģ[G] v‚āā` for the formal reading, and
--   `Adjacent G v‚āĀ v‚āā` is what we want for the prose.
--
-- * **No new arrow notation.** The LN reserves arrow notation for
--   the six primitives of def_3_2; def_3_3 introduces *English*
--   names for compositions of those primitives. Mirroring that
--   editorial choice, we keep the def_3_2 arrows as the formal-
--   syntax layer and these new identifiers as the prose-syntax
--   layer.
/-- `v‚āĀ` and `v‚āā` are *adjacent in `G`*: there is some edge of any
orientation between them. Definitionally `sus G v‚āĀ v‚āā` from
def_3_2; the readable name exists so downstream prose statements
such as claim 3.1's "no two nodes in $J$ are adjacent" can be cited
without unfolding to `sus`. -/
lean.statement:
def Adjacent (G : CDMG őĪ) (v‚āĀ v‚āā : őĪ) : Prop := sus G v‚āĀ v‚āā
@[simp] theorem adjacent_iff {G : CDMG őĪ} {v‚āĀ v‚āā : őĪ} :
    Adjacent G v‚āĀ v‚āā ‚ÜĒ sus G v‚āĀ v‚āā := Iff.rfl

lean.title: EdgeRelations -- edge into v‚āĀ
lean.comments:
--
-- `EdgeInto G v‚āĀ v‚āā` means the edge between `v‚āĀ` and `v‚āā` has an
-- arrowhead at `v‚āĀ`. The two LN spellings of this -- `v‚āĀ \hut v‚āā`
-- and `v‚āĀ \huh v‚āā` -- are exactly the disjuncts of `hus` from
-- def_3_2. Item-2's second sentence ("edges of the form
-- $v_1 \tuh v_2$ or $v_1 \huh v_2$ are called into $v_2$") uses the
-- same predicate with the arguments swapped: "edge into $v_2$" is
-- `EdgeInto G v‚āā v‚āĀ`. No second predicate is introduced.
/-
Verbatim from `lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex` (def 3.3,
item 2):

  Edges of the form $v_1 \hut v_2$ or $v_1 \huh v_2$ are called
  \emph{into $v_1$}.  \\
        Edges of the form $v_1 \tuh v_2$ or $v_1 \huh v_2$ are called
  \emph{into $v_2$}.
-/
--
-- ## Design choice
--
-- * **Convention: arrowhead at the *first* argument.** The LN uses
--   "into $v_1$" with $v_1$ as the first vertex listed, and `hus`
--   (the def_3_2 underlying form) also picks out the $v_1$
--   endpoint as the arrowhead site. By matching that convention,
--   `EdgeInto G v‚āĀ v‚āā` reads as "an edge into $v_1$, between $v_1$
--   and $v_2$". The LN's "into $v_2$" sentence is the same
--   predicate with arguments swapped: `EdgeInto G v‚āā v‚āĀ`.
--
-- * **`def`, not `abbrev` or new notation.** Same reasoning as for
--   `Adjacent`: this is prose terminology, and def_3_2's
--   `v‚āĀ ‚á∑[G] v‚āā` already covers the formal phrasing.
/-- `EdgeInto G v‚āĀ v‚āā` -- the edge between `v‚āĀ` and `v‚āā` is *into
`v‚āĀ`*, i.e. has an arrowhead at `v‚āĀ` (either a directed edge
`v‚āĀ ‚üĶ[G] v‚āā` or a bidirected edge `v‚āĀ ‚ü∑[G] v‚āā`). Definitionally
`hus G v‚āĀ v‚āā` from def_3_2. -/
lean.statement:
def EdgeInto (G : CDMG őĪ) (v‚āĀ v‚āā : őĪ) : Prop := hus G v‚āĀ v‚āā
@[simp] theorem edgeInto_iff {G : CDMG őĪ} {v‚āĀ v‚āā : őĪ} :
    EdgeInto G v‚āĀ v‚āā ‚ÜĒ hus G v‚āĀ v‚āā := Iff.rfl

lean.title: EdgeRelations -- edge out of v‚āĀ
lean.comments:
--
-- `EdgeOutOf G v‚āĀ v‚āā` means there is a *directed* edge with `v‚āĀ`
-- as the tail, i.e. `(v‚āĀ, v‚āā) ‚ąą G.E`, equivalently `tuh G v‚āĀ v‚āā`
-- from def_3_2. The LN gives two equivalent spellings of this same
-- condition: `v‚āĀ \tuh v‚āā` and `v‚āā \hut v‚āĀ` -- both unfold to the
-- same `G.E` membership. `edgeOutOf_iff_hut` below makes that
-- equivalence explicit.
/-
Verbatim from `lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex` (def 3.3,
item 3):

  Edges of the form $v_1 \tuh v_2$ or $v_2 \hut v_1$ are called
  \emph{out of $v_1$}.
-/
--
-- ## Design choice
--
-- * **Bidirected edges are deliberately excluded.** A bidirected
--   edge $v_1 \huh v_2$ has arrowheads at *both* endpoints, so it
--   is "into" both vertices but "out of" neither. The LN encodes
--   that asymmetry between items 2 and 3 by listing `\huh` only on
--   the "into" side, never on the "out of" side. This is load-
--   bearing downstream: directed walks (def_3_4) and the
--   parents/ancestors relations (def_3_5) all condition on directed
--   (`\tuh`) edges only, never bidirected ones.
--
-- * **The LN's two spellings collapse to one.** $v_1 \tuh v_2$ and
--   $v_2 \hut v_1$ are *equal* propositions (both unfold to
--   $(v_1, v_2) \in G.E$); the LN gives both spellings to tell the
--   reader they may pick whichever orientation reads better in a
--   given context. We pick `tuh G v‚āĀ v‚āā` as the canonical form and
--   record the alternative spelling as `edgeOutOf_iff_hut` below.
--
-- * **`def`, not `abbrev` or new notation.** Same reasoning as the
--   two previous items.
/-- `EdgeOutOf G v‚āĀ v‚āā` -- the edge between `v‚āĀ` and `v‚āā` is *out
of `v‚āĀ`*: a directed edge `v‚āĀ ‚ü∂[G] v‚āā`. Definitionally
`tuh G v‚āĀ v‚āā` from def_3_2. Note the deliberate asymmetry with
`EdgeInto`: bidirected edges are excluded here, because a
bidirected edge has arrowheads at both endpoints and so is "into"
both vertices but "out of" neither. -/
lean.statement:
def EdgeOutOf (G : CDMG őĪ) (v‚āĀ v‚āā : őĪ) : Prop := tuh G v‚āĀ v‚āā
@[simp] theorem edgeOutOf_iff {G : CDMG őĪ} {v‚āĀ v‚āā : őĪ} :
    EdgeOutOf G v‚āĀ v‚āā ‚ÜĒ tuh G v‚āĀ v‚āā := Iff.rfl
theorem edgeOutOf_iff_hut (G : CDMG őĪ) (v‚āĀ v‚āā : őĪ) :
    EdgeOutOf G v‚āĀ v‚āā ‚ÜĒ hut G v‚āā v‚āĀ := Iff.rfl
theorem Adjacent.symm {G : CDMG őĪ} {v‚āĀ v‚āā : őĪ}
    (h : Adjacent G v‚āĀ v‚āā) : Adjacent G v‚āā v‚āĀ := by
  rcases h with htuh | hhut | hhuh
  ¬∑ exact Or.inr (Or.inl htuh)
  ¬∑ exact Or.inl hhut
  ¬∑ exact Or.inr (Or.inr (G.L_symm hhuh))

lean_explanation:
The formalisation introduces three prose-level predicates on a `CDMG őĪ`, each a thin wrapper around a primitive from def_3_2: `Adjacent G v‚āĀ v‚āā := sus G v‚āĀ v‚āā`, `EdgeInto G v‚āĀ v‚āā := hus G v‚āĀ v‚āā`, and `EdgeOutOf G v‚āĀ v‚āā := tuh G v‚āĀ v‚āā`. Each wrapper is paired with a `@[simp]` characterisation lemma (`adjacent_iff`, `edgeInto_iff`, `edgeOutOf_iff`) proved by `Iff.rfl`, so the names exist for readability in goal states yet `simp` can unfold them transparently when needed.

There is one structural divergence from the LaTeX: the LN gives two grammatical sentences for "into" (an arrowhead at $v_1$ and an arrowhead at $v_2$), but only one Lean predicate is defined; the second sentence is captured by swapping arguments, i.e. `EdgeInto G v‚āā v‚āĀ`. Similarly, the LN's two spellings `v‚āĀ ‚ü∂ v‚āā` and `v‚āā ‚üĶ v‚āĀ` for "out of $v_1$" collapse to one Lean definition, with the alternative spelling exposed as `edgeOutOf_iff_hut : EdgeOutOf G v‚āĀ v‚āā ‚ÜĒ hut G v‚āā v‚āĀ`, again `Iff.rfl`.

Beyond the wrappers, `Adjacent.symm` is proved by `rcases` on the underlying `sus` disjunction (`tuh`, `hut`, `huh`), reassembling the symmetric witness and invoking `G.L_symm` on the bidirected branch to flip the unordered $L$-edge. The naming `hus`, `tuh`, `hut`, `huh` is inherited from def_3_2 and encodes head/tail markings at the two endpoints.
design_choices:
The wrappers are written as `def` rather than `abbrev` or `notation`. The comments justify this on two grounds: a `def` produces a stable identifier that survives into goals, error messages, and prose-style claim statements (e.g. claim 3.1's "no two nodes in $J$ are adjacent"), whereas `abbrev` would immediately unfold to `sus` and lose the readable name; and `notation` would introduce a new arrow token, which is unnecessary because def_3_2 already provides arrow notation such as `v‚āĀ ‚Üģ[G] v‚āā` for the formal layer. The intent is to keep def_3_2's arrows as the formal-syntax layer and these identifiers as the prose-syntax layer.

For `EdgeInto`, the convention is that the arrowhead sits at the *first* argument, matching the LN's "into $v_1$" phrasing and the underlying `hus`, so the LN's "into $v_2$" sentence becomes the same predicate with arguments swapped.

For `EdgeOutOf`, bidirected edges are deliberately excluded: a bidirected edge $v_1 \huh v_2$ has arrowheads at both endpoints, so it is "into" both vertices but "out of" neither. The comment flags this as load-bearing for downstream definitions (directed walks in def_3_4, parents/ancestors in def_3_5), which condition only on directed `tuh` edges. The two LN spellings `v‚āĀ \tuh v‚āā` and `v‚āā \hut v‚āĀ` are noted to be equal propositions; `tuh G v‚āĀ v‚āā` is picked as canonical and the alternative is recorded as `edgeOutOf_iff_hut`.
